pattern_recognition: Binary Search; this problem can be categorized under search algorithms as it involves finding a specific version among a sorted range. The signals include the need to minimize API calls while determining the first bad version.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check each version sequentially from 1 to n until the first bad version is found.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through each version and call isBadVersion(i) until the first bad version is identified.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize binary search to efficiently narrow down the range of versions by halving the search space with each API call.
approaches.algorithm: Set two pointers, left and right, and repeatedly check the middle version. Adjust the pointers based on whether the middle version is bad or not.

dry_run: For n = 5 with versions [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] where version 4 is the first bad version: Start with left = 1 and right = 5. Check mid = 3: isBadVersion(3) returns false, so move left to 4. Now check mid = 4: isBadVersion(4) returns true, so move right to 4. Finally, check mid = 4 again, confirming it's the first bad version.
edge_cases: If n = 0 (no versions), the function should handle this gracefully. If n = 1, it should correctly identify whether that single version is bad. If there are duplicates in the versions, ensure the first bad version is found correctly. Large inputs should be handled efficiently without exceeding limits.
common_pitfalls: 1) Not accounting for the case where all versions are good; ensure the function can handle this. 2) Misunderstanding the API's behavior; ensure isBadVersion(i) is called correctly. 3) Failing to minimize API calls; avoid unnecessary checks by implementing an efficient search strategy.
